headline: Ohio State's Ground Game Overwhelms Michigan in Statement Win
body: Ohio State arrived in Ann Arbor with a clear operational blueprint and executed it without mercy. The Buckeyes controlled the line of scrimmage from the opening kickoff, amassing 236 rushing yards on the strength of a committee approach led by Donaldson's 83 yards. That ground dominance set the tone for everything that followed, allowing Julian Sayin to operate in clean pockets and make decisive throws downfield. Sayin's four touchdown passes were the byproduct of a Michigan defense that couldn't generate consistent pressure and had to respect the run game at all times.

Michigan's offense sputtered throughout. Browne completed only 60 percent of his passes for 131 yards, and the Wolverines' rushing attack managed just 98 yards despite Wendtland's industrious effort. The telling stat came early: Michigan's lone turnover proved costly in a game where field position and rhythm were essential. Port and the receiving corps couldn't establish consistent separation, leaving Browne to work within a shrinking window. By the fourth quarter, the game had already been decided.

This wasn't a shootout or a defensive clinic. It was one team imposing its will in the trenches and another team unable to match that intensity. Ohio State's offensive line gave Sayin time to work through progressions, and when Michigan stacked the box to stop the run, the Buckeyes simply threw it over the top. That balance, executed cleanly and without panic, is what separated these teams on a September afternoon in the Big House.
turningPoint: Ohio State's ground game wearing down Michigan's defense by halftime made the Buckeyes' passing lanes wider in the second half, turning what could have been competitive into a comfortable margin.

unsungHero.name: Donaldson
unsungHero.why: His 83 yards established the physical tone that allowed Sayin's passing game to thrive, yet he won't be remembered the way the four-touchdown QB will be
